params.text:
Why food safety and nutrition matter in care

People who draw on care and support are often more vulnerable to the effects of unsafe food. Older adults, people who are frail, and those living with long-term conditions or weakened immune systems can become seriously ill from foodborne illness that a healthy adult might shrug off. At the same time, good nutrition and hydration are central to a person's health, dignity and wellbeing.

Getting food right is both a safety issue and a quality-of-life issue. Poor practice can lead to sickness, hospital admissions and, in the worst cases, death. Good practice helps people stay well, recover faster and enjoy their food.

  • Foodborne illness (food poisoning) can cause vomiting, diarrhoea, dehydration and serious complications in vulnerable people.
  • Malnutrition and dehydration are common and often unrecognised in care settings, yet largely preventable.
  • Supporting people to eat and drink well respects their choices, culture and independence.

The law and the regulator

Food safety in England is underpinned by the Food Safety Act 1990 and the Food Hygiene (England) Regulations 2006, which together make it an offence to supply food that is unsafe or harmful to health. The Food Standards Agency (FSA) is the independent government body responsible for protecting public health in relation to food.

The Care Quality Commission (CQC) inspects care services and expects providers to meet people's nutritional and hydration needs safely. Where food is prepared for people, services must show they manage food safety properly and support people to eat and drink well.

params.question: People receiving care are generally more vulnerable to serious harm from unsafe food than a healthy adult.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Not quite. Vulnerable people are at greater risk from foodborne illness, which is why safe food handling matters so much in care.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. Frailty, age and long-term conditions all increase the risk of serious illness from contaminated food.
